name: The Sicilian Defense as Black
description: A Black repertoire for the Sicilian Defense, drilled one position at a time: every card is a position with exactly one book move to find. Built from 3,000,000 rated games (Lichess database 2026-06, ratings 1600-2200): opponent replies branch by how often they are actually played, covering the most common 90% at each turn, so frequent lines run deep and rare sidelines stay shallow. 120 positions.

fields.prompt: You are Black and want to play the Sicilian Defense. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 1...c5 fights for d4 from the wing and unbalances the position immediately, the most combative reply to 1. e4.
The defining position of the Sicilian. Black stakes a wing pawn against White's center and steers the game away from symmetrical 1...e5 territory.

fields.front: After 2. Nf3, name Black's three main second moves.
fields.extra: 2...d6 heads for the Najdorf and Dragon, 2...Nc6 for the Sveshnikov and Classical, and 2...e6 for the Taimanov and Kan families.

fields.prompt: You are Black, heading for a Najdorf or Dragon. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 2...d6 controls e5 so a later ...Nf6 cannot be chased by e4-e5, and keeps both 5...a6 (Najdorf) and 5...g6 (Dragon) available.

fields.prompt: You are Black, heading for a Sveshnikov or Classical. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 2...Nc6 develops the knight to its best square and keeps ...e5 ideas in reserve. It is the move order that unlocks the Sveshnikov, at the cost of allowing the Rossolimo 3. Bb5.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Open Sicilian. What is the book move?
fields.extra: Take it: the capture trades a wing pawn for a center pawn and half-opens the c-file. Declining just lets White keep the big center.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Open Sicilian. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 4...Nf6 develops with an attack on e4, forcing 5. Nc3 and denying White the c4 clamp (the Maroczy Bind). Almost every Sicilian runs through this move.

fields.front: The Open Sicilian crossroads after 5. Nc3. Which variations can Black choose between with the next move?
fields.extra: One position, four openings. Black's fifth move sets the character of the whole game.

fields.prompt: You are Black and want the Najdorf. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 5...a6 takes b5 from White's knights and bishop, preparing ...e5 and ...b5. The most popular chess opening position in history.
The little pawn move to a6 defines the Najdorf, the lifetime weapon of Fischer and Kasparov.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf, Opocensky Variation. What is the book move?
fields.extra: With b5 already covered by ...a6, Black grabs the center. The backward d6 pawn is hard to attack, and Black gets active play on e5 and the queenside.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf, Opocensky Variation. What is the book move?
fields.extra: Simple chess: ...Be7 and ...O-O first, then ...Be6 and ...Nbd7. The backward d6 pawn looks like a target but is almost impossible for White to get at.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 6...e6 7. f4 is the old main line; the famous Poisoned Pawn arises after 7...Qb6, when Black grabs b2 and hangs on.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf, choosing the classical line over the Poisoned Pawn. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 7...Be7 calmly takes the sting out of the pin and prepares ...Qc7, ...Nbd7, and ...b5. The wild alternative is the Poisoned Pawn, 7...Qb6 hitting b2.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf, Fischer-Sozin Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 6...e6 blunts the bishop's a2-g8 diagonal. Black follows up with ...b5, ...Bb7, and pressure against e4.

fields.front: Najdorf: White plays 6. Be3, intending f3, Qd2, and O-O-O. What is this system called?
fields.extra: White castles long and storms the kingside with g4-g5; Black counters with ...e5, ...Be6, and queenside play. The modern main battleground of the Najdorf.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf, English Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 6...e5 boots the d4 knight and claims the center before White's f3, Qd2, and O-O-O machine gets rolling. The d5 hole is the price; space and time are the payoff.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf, English Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 7...Be6 develops toward c4, supports a later ...d5 break, and clears the way for ...Nbd7 and ...b5. Black's play comes on the queenside, exactly where White's king is headed.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Najdorf, English Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 8...Be7 prepares ...O-O and ...Nbd7 while keeping ...d5 in reserve; the modern alternative is 8...h5, stopping g4 before it starts.

fields.prompt: You are Black and want the Dragon. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 5...g6 prepares the fianchetto: the bishop goes to g7 and rakes the long diagonal toward White's queenside.
Named for the pawn structure's resemblance to the Draco constellation. Black's whole game revolves around the g7 bishop.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Dragon vs the Yugoslav Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: Finish the fianchetto: the g7 bishop is the Dragon's reason for being, raking the a1-h8 diagonal at White's queenside. Everything Black does flows through this piece.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Dragon vs the Yugoslav Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: Castling into the coming pawn storm looks brave, but the king needs shelter and the rooks must connect; ...Nc6, ...Bd7, and ...Rc8 arrive in time to counterattack.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Dragon vs the Yugoslav Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 8...Nc6 pressures d4 and prepares ...Bd7 and ...Rc8, so that every Black piece points at White's queenside before the h-pawn storm lands.

fields.front: Against the Dragon, White has set up Be3, f3, Qd2, and Bc4, ready to castle long. Name this setup.
fields.extra: White's most critical anti-Dragon weapon: O-O-O, then h4-h5 to pry open the h-file while f3 secures e4 and g4.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Dragon vs the Yugoslav Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 9...Bd7 connects the rooks so ...Rc8 comes next; the standard regrouping ...Ne5-c4 then offers to trade off White's attacking pieces.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Dragon vs the Yugoslav Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: The rook takes the half-open c-file, aiming at c3, where the thematic exchange sacrifice ...Rxc3 rips open White's king. The race is joined: ...Ne5-c4 versus h4-h5.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Dragon vs the Yugoslav Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: With no bishop on c4 to watch d5, Black frees the game immediately: 9...d5 blasts open the center before White's kingside pawns move an inch.

fields.front: Black fianchettoes on move four, skipping ...d6. Which variation?
fields.extra: By omitting ...d6, Black hopes to play ...d5 in one move and save a tempo on the normal Dragon. The drawback: White can clamp with 5. c4.

fields.front: White meets the Accelerated Dragon with 5. c4. What is this pawn formation called?
fields.extra: Pawns on c4 and e4 clamp the d5 square, denying Black the freeing break. Black stays solid but cramped and plays for exchanges and the ...b5 lever.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Accelerated Dragon vs the Maroczy Bind. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 5...Nf6 develops with a hit on e4, forcing Nc3. Then Black stays patient: ...d6, ...Bg7, exchanges on d4, and the ...b5 lever to chip at the bind.

fields.prompt: You are Black and want the Sveshnikov. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 5...e5 punches the d4 knight and stakes out the center immediately, accepting the d5 hole as the price of activity.
Reached via 2...Nc6, not 2...d6. Once considered anti-positional for the d5 hole, now bedrock theory and a world championship main weapon.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Sveshnikov. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 6...d6 denies the knight its dream check on d6 and shores up e5. The b5 knight now bites on granite and will be driven to the rim with ...a6.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Sveshnikov. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 7...a6 puts the question to the b5 knight, whose only retreat is the rim square a3. Black gains the tempo needed for ...b5 and full queenside expansion.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Sveshnikov. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 8...b5 shuts the a3 knight out of c4 and grabs queenside space. White's main tries are 9. Bxf6 and 9. Nd5.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Sveshnikov. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 9...Be7 breaks the pin on the spot; after 10. Bxf6 Bxf6 Black holds the bishop pair, and the f6 bishop reroutes via g5 to fight for the dark squares.

fields.prompt: You are Black in the Sveshnikov. What is the book move?
fields.extra: Take with the pawn: 9...gxf6 keeps the fight for d5 alive because ...f5 comes next, hitting e4 and unbalancing the game. 10. Nd5 f5 is the great Sveshnikov tabiya.

fields.prompt: You are Black and want the Classical Variation. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 5...Nc6 puts the second knight on its natural square and commits to nothing: Black can still choose ...e6, ...e5, or ...g6 structures depending on White's setup.
Both knights on their natural squares, everything kept flexible. Black can still steer toward Scheveningen or Boleslavsky structures.

fields.front: White answers the Classical with 6. Bg5, preparing Qd2 and O-O-O. Name this system.
fields.extra: White's main try against the Classical: pressure on f6, long castling, and the threat of doubling Black's pawns at the right moment. Black's main reply is 6...e6.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Richter-Rauzer Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 6...e6 builds the compact small center and prepares ...Be7 or the modern ...a6 and ...Bd7 scheme; Black waits to see White's plan before committing the king.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Richter-Rauzer Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 7...a6 rules out Ndb5 and Bb5 tricks and prepares ...Bd7 and ...b5. In the Rauzer, Black often leaves the king in the center and attacks first.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Richter-Rauzer Attack. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 8...Bd7 completes the queenside plan: ...Rc8 and ...b5 follow, with pressure building against c3 and White's king.
White intends d4 with a pawn recapture, dodging Open Sicilian theory. Respectable and popular at every level.

fields.front: How do you meet the Alapin's 2. c3?
fields.extra: Both strike at e4 immediately. 2...Nf6 provokes 3. e5 Nd5, and 2...d5 3. exd5 Qxd5 is safe because c3 has taken the b1 knight's square, so the queen cannot be hit by Nc3.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Alapin. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 3...Nd5 is the whole point of 2...Nf6: with a pawn on c3, White has no Nc3 to challenge the knight, so it sits securely in the center.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Alapin. What is the book move?
fields.extra: Trade the wing pawn for the center pawn, Sicilian style. Black will gang up on the d4 and e5 pawns with ...Nc6 and ...d6 before White consolidates.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Alapin. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 5...Nc6 keeps the pressure on d4 and adds a hit on e5. Develop first; the strike ...d6 against White's center comes next.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Alapin. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 6...d6 attacks the e5 spearhead at once; after it liquidates, White is left nursing an isolated or overextended d4 pawn for the rest of the game.
White develops with tempo and is ready to castle, threatening to inflict doubled pawns with Bxc6. The main way strong players dodge the Sveshnikov.

fields.front: How do you meet the Rossolimo's 3. Bb5?
fields.extra: The main line. Black accepts doubled pawns after Bxc6 dxc6 in exchange for the bishop pair and a dark-square grip. 3...e6 and 3...d6 are also fully respectable.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Rossolimo. What is the book move?
fields.extra: 4...dxc6 recaptures toward the center, opens the c8 bishop, and hands Black the bishop pair as full payment for the doubled pawns.

fields.prompt: You are Black vs the Rossolimo. What is the book move?
fields.extra: Complete the fianchetto. With ...Nf6 or ...e5 to follow, Black's dark-square grip and two bishops give a comfortable, strategically rich game.
